abstract: This study sought to identify the core knowledge and competencies essential for executive leadership in higher education from the perspective of sitting university presidents. Through in-depth interviews, thirteen presidents—all holding doctoral degrees in higher education administration—reflected on the abilities required to navigate the complexities of the 21st-century presidency. The participants categorized essential knowledge into three primary domains: foundational (history and finance), cultural (context and public image), and complex cognitive thinking. In terms of practical competencies, the respondents emphasized the importance of personal attributes, management (including fundraising, enrollment, and strategic planning), and multifaceted communication skills. The findings reveal that modern presidents view their roles as a hybrid of visionary leadership and pragmatic management, suggesting that specialized academic preparation and professional development are vital for success in these high-visibility positions.
